# Testing the Ability of Teachers and Students to Differentiate between Essays Generated by ChatGPT and High School Students

- **Source URL:** https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10493551
- **Snapshot method:** NSF accepted-manuscript PDF text extraction
- **Retrieved:** 2026-05-05

## Article Body

Hindawi
Human Behavior and Emerging Technologies
Volume 2023, Article ID 1923981, 9 pages
https://doi.org/10.1155/2023/1923981

Research Article
Testing the Ability of Teachers and Students to
Differentiate between Essays Generated by ChatGPT and High
School Students

         Tal Waltzer ,1 Riley L. Cox ,1,2 and Gail D. Heyman                                1

         1
             Department of Psychology, University of California San Diego, La Jolla CA 92093, USA
         2
             Cathedral Catholic High School, San Diego CA 92130, USA

         Correspondence should be addressed to Gail D. Heyman; gheyman@ucsd.edu

         Received 3 April 2023; Revised 23 April 2023; Accepted 29 April 2023; Published 26 June 2023

         Academic Editor: Zheng Yan

         Copyright © 2023 Tal Waltzer et al. This is an open access article distributed under the Creative Commons Attribution License,
         which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

         The release of ChatGPT in late 2022 prompted widespread concern about the implications of artiﬁcial intelligence for academic
         integrity, but thus far there has been little direct empirical evidence to inform this debate. Participants (69 high school teachers,
         140 high school students, total N = 209) took an AI Identiﬁcation Test in which they read pairs of essays—one written by a high
         school student and the other by ChatGPT—and guessed which was generated by the chatbot. Accuracy was only 70% for teachers,
         and it was slightly worse for students (62%). Self-reported conﬁdence did not predict accuracy, nor did experience with ChatGPT
         or subject-matter expertise. Well-written student essays were especially hard to diﬀerentiate from the ChatGPT texts. In another
         set of measures, students reported greater optimism than their teachers did about the future role of ChatGPT in education.
         Students expressed disapproval of submitting ChatGPT-generated essays as one’s own but rated this and other possible
         academic integrity violations involving ChatGPT less negatively than teachers did. These results form an empirical basis for
         further work on the relationship between AI and academic integrity.

                                                        Teacher
                                                        Student
                           Direct: A student uses Chat GPT to write an essay for them and submits the direct generated answer.
                           Modify: A student uses Chat GPT to write an essay for their class, then the student edits the output and submits the revised essay.
                           Enhance: A student writes an essay, asks Chat GPT to improve the essay’s vocabulary or structure, and then submits the exact revision.
                           Format: A student writes an essay, then uses Chat GPT to change it into a specifc format (e.g., MLA, APA) and submits the output.
                           Practice: A student uses Chat GPT to generate practice problems while studying for class.

about using ChatGPT to improve an essay’s vocabulary             and weaknesses, their hallmarks and blind spots — in order to
before submitting it: on average, students viewed this as        work alongside them” [42].
good, and teachers viewed it as bad. Future work will be              Understanding how people think about the role of
needed to understand the reason for this diﬀerence. Such         ChatGPT in educational contexts is important not only due
work could draw on the extended Technology Acceptance            to its implications for student learning but for broader moral
Model [21, 23, 44], which examines factors such as the per-      questions as well, including fairness. As one student com-
ceived usefulness of speciﬁc technologies and how much           mented, “I’m concerned because people who don’t use AI, like
users report trusting them. It will also be important to         me, will see their education and grades suﬀer if it becomes nor-
explore the eﬀects of people’s prior experiences with related    mal.” These kinds of risks, along with the potential beneﬁts,
technologies [45].                                               require creative problem-solving to eﬀectively address. Empir-
     This research has a number of limitations. We tested        ical evidence and vigorous debate will play a critical role in
only a narrow range of essays in one subject area, and more      navigating this new educational landscape.
work is needed to determine the generalizability of the
results. It is also unclear how certain methodological deci-     6. Conclusion
sions aﬀected the results, such as our decision to use a word
processor to correct obvious spelling and grammar errors in      The present research found that high school teachers were
the student essays. In addition, it will be important to exam-   slightly better than high school students at distinguishing
ine how providing ChatGPT with additional information in         between essays generated by ChatGPT versus high school
the prompt aﬀects performance (e.g., asking it to write like a   students. However, even the teachers had considerable trou-
high school student or avoid certain transition words).          ble with this task, especially when the students’ essays were
     It is important not to overinterpret our ﬁndings. For       well written. Teachers who were more conﬁdent in their
example, the fact that experience with using ChatGPT did         ability to recognize ChatGPT-generated writing did no bet-
not appear to make a diﬀerence in accuracy should not be         ter than their counterparts who were less conﬁdent, and
interpreted to mean that experience with ChatGPT cannot          experience with ChatGPT and content-relevant expertise
be used to improve one’s ability to identify writing samples     was unrelated to this ability as well. Our attitude assessments
that have been produced by ChatGPT. It may be that spe-          showed that high school students had a generally negative
cialized training can be developed to help people pick up        view of submitting essays written by ChatGPT, but they
on cues associated with its writing style. Special software is   viewed doing this, as well as other potential violations of aca-
now being developed for this purpose, but this will not be       demic integrity, less negatively than the teachers did. Stu-
a panacea given that there will always be false positives and    dents were more likely than teachers to report being
false negatives [46, 47]. Moreover, focusing on superﬁcial       optimistic that ChatGPT will have beneﬁts for education.
indicators of AI-generated writing instead of learning out-      Taken together, our ﬁndings suggest that ChatGPT does
comes could lead to a counterproductive “arms race” in           pose a threat to academic integrity in high school, one that
which students try to modify their writing styles to seem less   calls for the development of new policies and social norms.
AI-like in response to teachers policing them.                   Given that AI will only become more sophisticated in the
     Our research shows that distinguishing student writing      future, our ﬁndings raise an array of important questions
from writing produced by chatbots such as ChatGPT is a sig-      about how educational systems can adjust to this powerful
niﬁcant challenge already, and it is likely to get even more     and rapidly advancing technology.
